summary: O'Neil examines the transformation of work from agricultural to manufacturing to what she calls 'the age of service,' questioning how this economic transition will function. She criticizes the celebration of improving jobs reports while ignoring that new jobs are often temporary, part-time, and lower-paying than the full-time jobs being lost. She proposes that the service economy operates like a pyramid scheme where the wealthy employ servants who then employ slightly less wealthy servants, requiring either extreme wealth concentration or alternative wealth transfers. The post questions whether economists have adequately explained this transition and wonders how care workers at the bottom of the pyramid will themselves be cared for in old age.
key_points: Monthly jobs reports mask the reality that new jobs are often temporary/part-time with lower wages than the full-time jobs being lost
The economy is transitioning from 'the age of stuff' (manufacturing) to 'the age of service' following the earlier shift from agriculture
The service economy functions as a pyramid scheme requiring either extreme wealth at the top or systematic wealth redistribution
Examples like concierge medicine and personal assistants for social calendars show this service economy already emerging
Most modern work including programming, writing, and teaching already qualifies as service industry work
money_quotes: The age of service is a kind of pyramid scheme where rich people employ individuals to service them in various ways, and then those people are paid well so they can hire slightly less rich people to service them, and so on.
I kind of want to complain at this point that economists are kind of useless when it comes to questions like this.
I feel like, when it comes to social meetings, if you have time to be social, you have time to arrange your social calendar.
The mathematician in me can't help but wonder, who will take care of the old people who used to be taking care of the old people?
Hell, the future could be pretty awesome if we arrange things well.
For this particular pyramid to work out, the rich have to be SUPER rich and they have to pay their servants very well indeed for the trickle down to work out.
key_insight: The transition to a service economy creates a pyramid structure that only functions with extreme wealth concentration at the top or deliberate wealth redistribution—neither of which current economic systems are designed to handle.
